coredata.dc:title: Reversible and color-variable afterglow luminescence of carbon dots triggered by water for multi-level encryption and decryption
coredata.dc:creator: Han, Zichen
Li, Peng
Deng, Yuchen
Li, Huanrong
coredata.dc:description: Stimulus-responsive smart afterglow materials with color-variable and reversible switch feature are urgently expected in view of actual application needs in the field of sensing, anti-counterfeiting, optoelectronic devices etc. Herein, we report a facile and effective construction strategy for preparing blue afterglow smart carbon dots nanomaterials (TPA-CDs/Si) by constructing Si-C covalent bonds within CDs utilizing silicon as doping element, displaying color-variable afterglow feature (blue to green) triggered by water owing to the formation of bridging hydrogen-bonded networks among TPA-CDs/Si nanocomponents connected by water molecule after the introduction of water. Such materials also realize reversible multi-level information encryption application via alternately adding water and heating owing to the formation and breaking of bridging hydrogen-bonded networks among TPA-CDs/Si nanocomponents in the presence and absence of water.
coredata.dcterms:subject: Stimulus response
Smart afterglow materials
Carbon dots
Color-variable
Multi-level information encryption

ce:caption.ce:simple-para: Characterization of TPA-CDs/Si. a) HR-TEM image, b) size distribution, c)SAED patterns and d) HR-TEM image.
a) XPS spectra of TPA-CDs/Si. b-d) HR XPS spectra of b) C 1 s, c) O 1 s, and d) Si 2p of TPA-CDs/Si and fitting results.
a) The steady-state fluorescence spectrum and b) afterglow spectrum of TPA-CDs/Si under excitation at 254 nm fitted by Gaussian function. c) The time-resolved afterglow spectra of TPA-CDs/Si.

a) Normalized fluorescence and afterglow emission spectra of TPA-CDs/Si under excitation at 254 nm. (inset: photographs of TPA-CDs/Si under sunlight, before and after turning off 254 nm UV light). b) Photographs of TPA-CDs/Si before and after adding water under sunlight, before and after turning off 254 nm UV light.

a) TEM image of TPA-CDs/Si@H2O. Inset: The size distribution from TEM image of TPA-CDs/Si@H2O. b) DSC-heating curves of TPA-CDs/Si@(H2O)x (x = 0, 0.1, 0.2) as a function of temperature. c) Schematic illustration of mutual transformation process of TPA-CDs/Si and TPA-CDs/Si@H2O.

a) Photographs of TPA-CDs/Si after adding different kinds of solvents under UV light (254 nm) on and off. (1-methanol, 2-ethanol, 3-dichloromethane (DCM), 4-dimethylsulfoxide (DMSO), 5-N-N dimethylformamide (DMF), 6-acetone, 7-Ammonium hydroxide (AH), 8-Triethylamine, 9-hexane, 10-ether, 11-petroleum ether (PE), 12-ethyl acetate (EA)). b)The afterglow emission intensity ratio at 424 nm to 492 nm of TPA-CDs/Si after adding water and heating.
Illustration of information encryption and decryption processes by using TPA-CD/Si via adding water and heating.
Afterglow lifetimes of TPA-CDs/Si fitted by four exponential function recorded at emission wavelength of 424 nm, 467 nm, 492 nm and 519 nm, respectively, under excitation at 254 nm.

head.ce:title: Reversible and color-variable afterglow luminescence of carbon dots triggered by water for multi-level encryption and decryption

ce:affiliation.ce:textfn: National-Local Joint Engineering Laboratory for Energy Conservation in Chemical Process Integration and Resources Utilization, Tianjin Key Laboratory of Chemical Process Safety, School of Chemical Engineering and Technology, Hebei University of Technology, GuangRong Dao 8, Hongqiao District, Tianjin 300130, PR China

sa:affiliation.sa:organization: National-Local Joint Engineering Laboratory for Energy Conservation in Chemical Process Integration and Resources Utilization
Tianjin Key Laboratory of Chemical Process Safety
School of Chemical Engineering and Technology

ce:affiliation.ce:source-text: National-Local Joint Engineering Laboratory for Energy Conservation in Chemical Process Integration and Resources Utilization, Tianjin Key Laboratory of Chemical Process Safety, School of Chemical Engineering and Technology, Hebei University of Technology, GuangRong Dao 8, Hongqiao District, Tianjin 300130, PR China

ce:abstract-sec.ce:simple-para: Stimulus-responsive smart afterglow materials with color-variable and reversible switch feature are urgently expected in view of actual application needs in the field of sensing, anti-counterfeiting, optoelectronic devices etc. Herein, we report a facile and effective construction strategy for preparing blue afterglow smart carbon dots nanomaterials (TPA-CDs/Si) by constructing Si-C covalent bonds within CDs utilizing silicon as doping element, displaying color-variable afterglow feature (blue to green) triggered by water owing to the formation of bridging hydrogen-bonded networks among TPA-CDs/Si nanocomponents connected by water molecule after the introduction of water. Such materials also realize reversible multi-level information encryption application via alternately adding water and heating owing to the formation and breaking of bridging hydrogen-bonded networks among TPA-CDs/Si nanocomponents in the presence and absence of water.

ce:section.ce:para: Stimulus-responsive smart luminescent materials have attracted tremendous interests owing to their important applications in various fields of sensing, anti-counterfeiting, optoelectronic devices and so on [1–4]. Conventional stimulus-responsive fluorescent systems are limited by background interference, and which are easily stolen, mimicked, or forged owing to the increasing familiarity of counterfeiters to the existing ones[5–9]. In view of practical applications, the development of novel smart materials with stimuli-responsive luminescence is urgently desirable, but remains a great challenge. With this objective, some stimulus-responsive organic afterglow materials have been recently designed by the regulation of molecular conformation and packing using crystallization engineering strategy [10–14]. Such materials possess superior performances, such as long-lived luminescence and high signal-to-noise ratio, allowing for the elimination of short-lived background fluorescence [15–18]. Importantly, the change in emission lifetime under external stimulus can also act as an additional visual monitoring parameter for smart luminescent materials, further enhancing the information security [19–22]. Consequently, stimulus-responsive afterglow materials are regarded as ideal candidates for security protection applications in information storage, encryption, and anti-counterfeiting [23–25]. Unfortunately, these materials frequently suffer from synthetic complexity, poor repeatability and processability, which may cause environmental pollution due to the use of organic solvents as well. Therefore, it is urgent to develop alternative stimulus-responsive afterglow systems via convenient and eco-friendly preparation strategy for high-level security protection applications.

Carbon dots (CDs) as emerging luminescent nanomaterials have attracted sustained attractions owing to their superior merits of low toxicity, inexpensive cost, excellent biocompatibility, facile preparation and superior optical properties [26–31]. Particularly, unique afterglow properties (room temperature phosphorescence and delayed fluorescence) have been achieved through stabilizing their excited triplet state by embedding in host matrix (such as polyvinyl alcohol, zeolites etc) or constructing self-protective CDs polymers [32–37]. However, nearly all of them exhibit afterglow properties only in the dry state owing to the quenching effect of dissolved oxygen and solvent assisted relaxation [38], impeding their practical application in high-tech fields. Recently, a handful of room-temperature long-afterglow materials in aqueous media are acquired by novel covalent bond grafting or hydrogen-bonded networks strategies [39–43]. Nevertheless, great majority of the aforementioned afterglow materials failed to display dynamic, color-variable and reversible water-stimulus-responsive performance, yet which could be as ideal candidates for high-level security protection applications.

To the best of our knowledge, different kinds of interaction forces have very important different influence on the afterglow features. For instance, Si-C covalent bonds interaction can promote the delayed fluorescence (DF) emission through effectively stabilizing the triplet excited state[39] and hydrogen-bonded networks are favor for the room temperature phosphorescence (RTP) emission in water dispersion[40,41]. Therefore, to acquire stimulus-responsive smart afterglow materials triggered by water, the coexistence of Si-C covalent bond and dynamic hydrogen-bonded networks (or dynamic Si-C covalent bond and hydrogen-bonded networks) tuned by water within CDs is a key and promising factor in the fabrication strategy. Herein, a facile and effective construction strategy for preparing blue afterglow smart materials (TPA-CDs/Si) with color-variable afterglow performance triggered by water is provided by constructing Si-C covalent bonds within CDs utilizing silicon as doping element. This unique phenomenon is realized through forming bridging hydrogen-bonded networks among TPA-CDs/Si nanocomponents connected by water molecule after the introduction water. Moreover, TPA-CDs/Si reveal reversible stimulus-responsive afterglow luminescence effect via alternately adding water and heating, which can be repeated for six cycles, manifesting their good recoverability. By taking advantages of these distinctive properties, advanced multi-level encryption are demonstrated.

All chemicals used were analytical reagent grade and were used as received without any further purification. KOH was purchased from Tianjin Huadong Reagent Factory (Tianjin, China). LUDOX(TM-40 colloidal silica) was purchased from ALDRICH (Shanghai, China). Terephthalic Acid was purchased from TCI (Shanghai, China). The double distilled water was produced by the laboratory water purification system (Simple-R015, ZHIANG) throughout the experiments.

The procedure was described as follows. LUDOX (20 g) was dispersed in deionized water (5 mL) under mechanical stirring followed by adding KOH solution (4.0 g KOH solid power and 15 mL deionized water). The mixtures were stirred and heated at 115 °C for 15 h. After that, the solution was transferred into a 50 mL Teflon-lined stainless-steel autoclave with adding terephthalic acid (0.4 g), and then heated to 170 °C for 10 h by hydrothermal treatment. The mother solution was produced via centrifuging the above solution at 11000 rpm, then the powder materials (TPA-CDs/Si) were obtained through spin-evaporation at 50 °C in rotary evaporator and calcination of the mother solutions at 200 °C for 6 h in a muffle furnace.
The detailed preparation process of TPA-CDs/Six (x = 0, 1, 5, 10) was similar with that of TPA-CDs/Si, yet the amount of LUDOX was changed to 0, 1, 5 and 10 g.
The hydrogel materials (TPA-CDs/Si@(H2O)x) were obtained by adding deionized water with different content in the TPA-CDs/Si powder materials.

The steady-state fluorescence and phosphorescence spectra of TPA-CDs/Si and TPA-CDs/Si@(H2O)x were tested on Edinburgh Instruments FS920P using a xenon arc lamp (Xe 900). The UV–vis spectra were performed on an Agilent cary100 spectrometer. X-ray photoelectron spectroscopy (XPS) was carried out on Perkin Elmzr PHI-1600 spectrometer. Transmission electron microscopy (TEM), Selected area electron diffraction (SAED) and Energy dispersive X-ray (EDX) mapping observations were performed on a Tecnai G2 F20 microscope. The rheology test was done on an AR 2000ex (TA Instrument) system. DSC was performed on a DSC Q-20 from TA Instruments using cooling and heating rates of 2 K min−1.

TPA-CDs/Si were hydrothermally synthesized using terephthalic acid (TPA) as carbon source and SiO2 as silicon source with alkaline environment at 170 °C for 6 h, which derived from their mother solutions followed by calcinating at 200 ℃ for 6 h. The successful preparation of CDs can be verified by well-dispersed quasispherical nanoparticles with an average size about 2.8 nm in transmission electron microscopy (TEM) image (Fig. 1 a and 1b). The selected area electron diffraction (SAED) pattern displays the crystalline structure of CDs (Fig. 1c). And the high resolution TEM (HRTEM) image reveals a well-resolved lattice spacing of 0.21 nm in CDs corresponding to the (100) facet of graphite carbon (Fig. 1d)[44,45]. Additionally, energy dispersive X-ray (EDX) mapping of TPA-CDs/Si shows uniform distribution of C and O elements and very lower content of Si elements (Fig. S1). This fact indicate that Si elements as doping element exist in TPA-CDs/Si, which can also be supported by the successful preparation of CDs without Si (TPA-CDs/Si0, Fig. S2). The chemical compositions and structure of TPA-CDs/Si are explored by Fourier transform infrared (FTIR) and X-ray photoelectron spectroscopy (XPS). As shown in Fig. S3, FTIR spectrum exhibits a strong absorption band at 3412 cm−1 attributed to the –OH stretching vibration and multiple weak absorption peaks at 2925–2975 cm−1 assigned to the C–H stretching vibration [46,47]. The peaks located at 1627 cm−1 and 1019 cm−1 are originated from C = O and C-O stretching vibrations [46,48,49]. The characteristic peaks at 1583 and 1437 cm−1 are also observed from the FT-IR spectrum, which can be ascribed to C = C group on the benzene ring [49,50]. These results can be further verified by the XPS analysis. As displayed in Fig. 2 a, the XPS spectrum contains three peaks at 284.0, 530.6, 379(293) and 102.0 eV belonging to C 1 s, O 1 s, K 2 s(K 2p) and Si 2p with corresponding contents to be 64.64%, 17.08%, 16.22% and 2.01%, respectively, specifying relatively low silicon-doping. The high-resolution (HR) XPS spectrum of C 1 s can be deconvoluted into three peaks at 284.6 (63.9%), 285.5 (12.6%) and 288.3 eV (23.5%), contributing to C–C/C = C, C-O and C = O bonds (Fig. 2b) [51,52]. More in detail, the O 1 s XPS spectrum exhibits two peaks at 530.4 (46.2%) and 531.4 eV (53.8%) for C = O and C-OH/C-O-C bonds (Fig. 2c) [48,49]. The Si 2p XPS imply the presence of Si-C (101.5 eV (54.5%)) and Si-O (102.4 eV (45.5%)) bonds in TPA-CDs/Si (Fig. 2d) [39,53,54]. Combining the above results, we speculate that TPA-CDs/Si are quasi-spherical carbogenic nanoparticles with crystallized sp2 carbon skeletons as core, which encompass diverse functional groups, such as C = O, C = C and C-Si etc.

As displayed in Fig. 3 a, TPA-CDs/Si shows a blue fluorescence centered at 407 nm, 425 nm and 474 nm under 254 nm UV lamp, while a blue afterglow can be seen by the naked eyes at room temperature after turning off the ultraviolet light (inset in Fig. 3b and Video S1). Their afterglow emission spectrum fitted by gaussian function and time-resolved afterglow spectra show multiple emissive centers at 424 nm, 467 nm, 492 nm and 519 nm (Fig. 3b-3c) [55,56]. And the lifetimes are fitted by four exponential function. These average lifetimes are calculated according to the following equation (Eq (1))[41,57], which fitted at 424 nm, 467 nm, 492 nm and 519 nm are 237.3 ms, 344.0 ms, 375.8 ms and 338.8 ms (Fig. S4a and Table 1 ) respectively, implying their triplet emission process [47]. And the afterglow quantum yields is 1.47%.
(1)
τ
avg
= ∑
α i
τ
i
2
/ ∑
α i
τ i

The emissive centers of 424 nm and 467 nm are attributed to delayed fluorescence (DF) owing to the high similarity with the fluorescence emission bands of 425 nm and 474 nm [39], while that of 492 nm and 519 nm are assigned to room temperature phosphorescence (RTP) due to large stokes shift compared with the aforementioned fluorescence emission bands [58,59]. To further ascertain the nature of DF, temperature-dependent afterglow spectra measurements for TPA-CDs/Si were recorded. We observed the afterglow intensities of 424 nm and 467 nm gradually decreased with increasing temperatures from 77 to 298 K (Fig. S4b), excluding the thermally assisted process [21,39]. Therefore, this DF could be attributed to a bimolecular triplet–triplet annihilation (TTA) process [60].

To make clarifications for the origin of fluorescence and afterglow emission, the absorption, fluorescence and afterglow excitation spectra of TPA-CDs/Si were investigated. As shown in Fig. S5a, their UV/Vis absorption spectrum contains absorption peaks entered at 225 nm, 263 nm attributed to π–π* transition of C = C bond and absorption peaks 281 nm, 355 nm assigned to n–π* transition of C = O bond, respectively [61,62]. The fluorescence excitation spectrum display multiple peaks at 273 nm and 320 nm (Fig. S5b), which overlap with the absorption band of C = C and C = O bonds, indicating that the observed blue fluorescence comes from the synergistic effect of π–π* transition of C = C and n–π* transition of C = O bond[34,48] and the emission center at 320 nm assigned to n–π* transition of C = O bond play a dominant position in their fluorescence excitation spectra. And the afterglow excitation spectrum display multiple peaks at 230 nm, 263 nm, 288 nm and 353 nm, which overlap with the absorption band of C = C and C = O bonds, indicating that the observed blue afterglow comes from the synergistic effect of π–π* transition of C = C and n–π* transition of C = O bond (Fig. S5c) [34,48]. Generally, the afterglow emission are mainly derived from the n–π* transition of C = O bonds rather than π–π* transition of C = C bonds owing to the lower excitation energy of the lowest singlet excited state (S1) for 1(n,π*) [59,63]. In order to explain the reason why the afterglow emission originate from π–π* transition of C = C bonds in this work, the nature of S1 state for 1(n,π*) and 1(π,π*) were measured according to their fluorescence spectra at low termperature of 77 K [63]. As shown in Fig. S6 and Table S1, there are similar lowest singlet excited state (S1) of C = O bonds (2.64 ev) and C = C bonds (2.68 eV) in TPA-CDs/Si system, making π–π* transition of C = C bonds become an efficient intermediate radiative pathway for T1 → S0 transition [63].

To further ascertain the afterglow generation mechanism of TPA-CDs/Si, we conducted a set of control experiments as follows. TPA-CDs/Si samples with different amounts of SiO2 were prepared, denoted as TPA-CDs/Six (x is the adding amount of SiO2 and the details are in Experimental Section, x = 0, 1, 5, 10 and 20). As displayed in HR XPS Si 2p spectra (Fig. 2d and Fig. S7), Si-C covalent bonds in TPA-CDs/Six appear and gradually increases with increasing adding amounts of SiO2. Meanwhile, the afterglow luminescence properties of TPA-CDs/Six have been systematically studied. We observed that TPA-CDs/Si0 display green afterglow emission centered at 469, 495 and 518 nm, and the emission center at 495 nm play a dominant position in their afterglow emission spectra (Fig. S8a). This may be owing to stabilizing triplet excited state caused by forming intraparticle hydrogen bonds [64]. Additionally, it is apparent that the enhancement of green afterglow emission of 469, 495 and 518 nm and the appearance of blue afterglow emission at 424 nm can be seen with increasing Si-C covalent bonds (Fig. S8b), which corresponds to the responding digital picture of TPA-CDs/Six samples after turning off 254 nm UV lamp by naked eye (Fig. S8c). These findings validly indicate that Si-C covalent bonds further stabilize the triplet excited state of DF (467 nm) and RTP (492 and 519 nm) and promote the formation of new triplet state of DF (424 nm).

To our knowledge, afterglow luminescence can be extensively quenched in the presence of water due to the quenching effect of dissolved oxygen and solvent assisted relaxation [38]. Surprisingly, a transition from blue to green afterglow for TPA-CDs/Si occurred by adding a certain amount of water (Fig. 4 and Video S2), evidencing their unusual water stimulus-responsive feature. To interpret the outstanding smart response characteristics, the afterglow spectra of TPA-CDs/Si@(H2O)x with different water contents were measured firstly. We found that there is an obvious decline of emissive center at 424 nm and some enhancement of emissive centers at 495 nm and 518 nm with water content of 20% (Fig. S9). Yet the intensity of three emissive centers has a certain degree of reduction with a further increase in the water content from 30% to 50%. To obtain in-depth insights of water stimulus-responsive feature, we made the analysis of data as follows. We observed that the stable hydrogel state of TPA-CDs/Si is formed after the introduction of water, which is supported by the following mechanical properties. As shown in Fig. S10, the storage moduli (G') was larger than loss moduli (G'') with increased oscillation strain from 0.1% to 100%, while G' remains larger than G'' over a wide angular frequency range (ω) from 0.05 to 100 rad s−1 under 1% strain, indicating the existence of the stable hydrogel state for TPA-CDs/Si@H2O[65–67]. These facts suggest that the formation of strong interaction between TPA-CDs/Si nanocomponents in the presence of water. Additionally, TEM images of TPA-CDs/Si display well-dispersed quasispherical nanoparticles with an increase average size about 6.1 nm after the introduction of water (recorded as TPA-CDs/Si@H2O, the size of TPA-CDs/Si is about 2.8 nm) (Fig. 5 a and 1b), indicating the formation of strong bridging interaction between TPA-CDs/Si nanocomponents connected water molecules. To further determine the nature of this strong bridging interaction, differential scanning calorimetry (DSC) measurement of TPA-CDs/Si@(H2O)x were investigated [40,68]. As shown in Fig. 5b, no obvious endothermic melting peak can be observed at a low water content of 10%, implying only non-freezing bound water exists in this portion. And a broad endothermic peak emerged as the water content gradually increased to 20%, illustrating the presence of two states of freezing water (freezing bound water and bulk water) and saturated non-freezing bound water in the composite. Additionally, the bulk water content wound continue growing with the increasing water content (from 30% to 50%) in TPA-CDs/Si@(H2O)x. The aforementioned results illustrated that non-freezing bound water is responsible for the water stimulus-responsive feature of TPA-CDs/Si, while bulk water has an adverse effect. To the best of our knowledge, non-freezing bound water can construct robust hydrogen-bonded networks between suitable organic molecules with carboxyl or hydroxyl group[40]. Thus, the bridging hydrogen-bonded networks among TPA-CDs/Si nanocomponents connected by water molecules was confirmed (the schematic diagram shown in Fig. 5c).

In view of the foregoing discussion, a possible mechanism is proposed as follows. TPA-CDs/Si display RTP emission peaks at 424 and 467 nm and DF emission peaks at 495 and 518 nm. The intraparticle hydrogen bonds in TPA-CDs/Si mainly stabilize the triplet excited states of RTP emission at 495 and 518 nm, while they also bring about DF emission at 467 nm [64]. And the formation of Si-C covalent bonds TPA-CDs/Si bring about the enhancement of green RTP emission of 495 and 518 nm and DF emission at 467 nm, while the appearance of blue DF emission at 424 nm can be seen. This is similar with the previous report [39,69], which is because that covalent bonds as a better alternative can bring about the coexistence of delayed fluorescence (DF) and room temperature phosphorescence (RTP) through stabilizing triplets. After introducing water, the bridging hydrogen-bonded networks among TPA-CDs/Si nanocomponents were formed, which can effectively stabilize the triplet states of emissive centers at 467 nm, 495 and 518 nm and inhibit the triplet states of emissive centers at 424 nm. This is mainly owing to the presence of competitive relationship between bridging hydrogen-bonded networks and Si-C covalent bond, which could be supported by comparative analysis of afterglow emission for TPA-CDs/Six with different Si content before and after adding water. As shown in Fig. S11, TPA-CDs/Six@H2O display the disappearance of the afterglow emission at 424 nm and the enhancement of the emission bands at 467 nm, 492 and 519 nm owing the formation of bridging hydrogen-bonded networks among TPA-CDs/Six nanocomponents connected by water molecules. The aforementioned fact suggest the presence of competitive relationship between bridging hydrogen-bonded networks and Si-C covalent bond. Notably, this stimulus-responsive feature cannot be observed with other common organic solvents (Fig. 6 a), indicating that only water can play this role here in forming bridging hydrogen-bonded networks among TPA-CDs/Si nanocomponents. Additionally, TPA-CDs/Si reveal reversible stimulus-responsive afterglow effect via alternately adding water and heating owing to the formation and breaking of the hydrogen-bonded networks in the presence and absence of water, which can be repeated for more than six cycles manifesting their good recoverability (Fig. 6b). Such interesting water stimulus-responsive feature endows TPA-CDs/Si with important potential for applications in advanced security encryption.

Benefited from the unique afterglow feature of TPA-CDs/Si, a multi-level encryption system was displayed. According to the model shown in Fig. 7 , a pattern of word “HEBUT” was prepared on the basis of TPA-CDs/Si powers. A visible blue fluorescence was seen by the naked eye under UV light irradiation of 254 nm. After turning off the UV lamp, the word “HEBUT” shows blue afterglow phenomenon with durations of several seconds. This is the first level of the encryption information. The letters “HE” demonstrates distinctive green afterglow after turning off the UV lamp when spraying a small amount of water on them, which is the second level of the encryption process. Notably, the blue afterglow can almost recovered after heating with a 600 ℃ heat gun for very short time of 10 s, which is considered as the decryption process.

In summary, we report stimulus-responsive smart afterglow carbon dots nanomaterials (TPA-CDs/Si) with color-variable and reversible switch feature by constructing Si-C covalent bonds within CDs utilizing silicon as doping element. TPA-CDs/Si display blue afterglow emission with multiple emissive centers containing delayed fluorescence at 424 nm, 467 nm and room temperature phosphorescence at 492 nm and 519 nm, originating from the synergistic effect of π–π* transition of C = C and n–π* transition of C = O bond. Surprisingly, TPA-CDs/Si reveal color-variable afterglow feature from blue to green triggered by water. This smart responsive characteristic is mainly because that the formation of bridging hydrogen-bonded networks among TPA-CDs/Si nanocomponents connected by water molecule after the introduction of water caused the obvious decline of emissive center at 424 nm and significant enhancement of emissive centers at 492 nm and 519 nm, resulting from the presence of competitive relationship between bridging hydrogen-bonded networks and Si-C covalent bond. Further, such materials realize reversible multi-level information encryption application via alternately adding water and heating owing to the formation and breaking of bridging hydrogen-bonded networks among TPA-CDs/Si nanocomponents in the presence and absence of water.

ce:conflict-of-interest.ce:para: The authors declare that they have no known competing financial interests or personal relationships that could have appeared to influence the work reported in this paper.

ce:acknowledgment.ce:para: Financial support by the National Natural Science Foundation of China (21171046, 21502039, 21271060), the Central Government Guiding Local science and Technology Development Fund Projects (No. 206Z1401G), the Natural Science Foundation of Hebei Province (No. B2020202002, B2016202147, B2016202149, B2017202048), Educational Committee of Hebei Province (LJRC021, QN2015172), Hebei Provincial College of Science and Technology Research Project (BJ2018054), Hebei Provincial Personnel Department Project (A2017010003), Tianjin Natural Science Foundation (18JCYBJC17200), National Natural Science Foundation of China (21802031), National Natural Science Foundation of Hebei Province (B2017202133), and Colleges and Universities in Hebei Province Science and Technology Research Project (QN2017046).
